title: Large Outdoor Bench with X-Back
summary: A porch-length bench with a lattice of mitered 2x2 Xs filling the back — the same repeating joint six times over, with the pocket holes deliberately hidden from the front.
description:
The back of this bench is a row of X's built from short mitered 2x2 pieces set between two vertical posts, and the one detail that separates a clean result from a sloppy one is entirely about hole placement, not cutting: every pocket hole in the lattice gets marked and labeled BEFORE drilling (letter each crossing joint — A to A, B to B — so you know which face of which piece needs the hole) specifically so no pocket hole ends up visible on the front of the bench. On the 45° mitered ends you can still drill a pocket hole even though there's less material to work with there; it holds fine despite looking marginal.

The back legs and back rail are the one place the plan asks you to build custom rather than to a fixed number: scribe them to fit by holding a long board in place against the actual back assembly and tracing the cut lines with a pencil, rather than trusting a cut-list dimension that assumes your frame came out exactly on spec.

The seat itself is a straightforward framed rectangle — 2x4 front, back, ends, and under-supports for mid-span stiffness under a bench this long — topped with four 2x6 slats. The arms cap the front legs, screwed from both the top and the back, and are wide enough to set a drink on without the bench feeling like a chair.

Build the seat frame and legs first and get them screwed together solid; the back lattice is decorative and structural bracing both, but it goes on last, once there's a stable base to build it up from.

tools.note: Every X-lattice piece gets a 45° cut.
Scribes the back legs and rail to fit the actual assembled frame.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: The X-back lattice and decorative verticals.

cutList.note: Treat as an estimate — scribe to fit by holding the board in place against the assembled back and tracing the cut lines.
Centered so there's 18-1/8" of space between each vertical and the back legs.
Both ends cut at 45°, ends ARE parallel, long point to long point measurement.
One end cut at 45°, longest point measurement.

steps.title: Build the back frame
steps.body: Join the back legs and back horizontal boards with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. If you'd rather work flat on a benchtop than reach around the back of the assembled bench later, you can add the decorative 2x2 verticals now instead of after the lattice step.

steps.title: Build the seat framing
steps.body: Assemble the seat frame — front, back, and ends — with either pocket holes or 2-1/2" self-tapping exterior screws, whichever you used for the back frame.

steps.title: Add the under supports and join the seat to the back
steps.body: Screw the two under-support pieces across the seat frame's mid-span — this is what keeps a bench this long from developing a bounce in the middle. Then attach the completed seat framing to the back frame with 2-1/2" exterior screws.

steps.title: Attach the front legs and arms
steps.body: Attach the front legs to the front of the seat framing with 2-1/2" screws. Add the arms on top, screwing from both the top and the back of each front leg for a solid, wobble-free connection.

steps.title: Lay the seat slats
steps.body: Attach the four 2x6 seat slats across the top of the seat frame, evenly spaced, with 2-1/2" screws into the frame and under-supports below.

steps.title: Build and fit the X-lattice
steps.body:
First, add the two 16"-long 2x2 decorative verticals to the back frame, centering them so there's 18-1/8" of open space between each vertical and the 2x4 back legs.

Then cut all the X-lattice pieces and dry-fit them into the openings. Once every piece fits, mark the pocket holes that need drilling on the BACK side of each piece, and letter the joints as you go (A-A on one crossing joint, B-B on the next, and so on) — this is what keeps you from accidentally drilling a hole that ends up facing the front of the bench. On the 45° mitered ends you can still drill a pocket hole; there isn't much material to work with, but it holds.

Attach every piece from the back side with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue.

images.alt: Long outdoor bench with an X-lattice back panel
